channel.title: Becoming Net Positive
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Becoming Net Positive

item.title: Why Are We Still Paying to Destroy the Ocean?
item.description:
For most of human history, the ocean seemed too vast to exhaust. Waste vanished into it. Fish seemed to return endlessly from it. The idea that humanity could destabilise the ocean itself, until remarkably recently, was essentially unimaginable.

Across cultures and millennia, people did not simply live beside the ocean, they lived in relationship to it. Societies revered it, feared it, prayed to it and filled it with gods. It fed them, carried them, drowned them, connected them, and shaped their creation stories.

The tragedy of the modern age is not that we stopped fearing the ocean, it is that we stopped respecting it. What once marked the edge of human power and understanding has been reduced to a route for commerce, a dump for waste, and a storehouse for protein on an industrial scale.

For many of us, the ocean crisis is something we understand but have not had to confront. During a recent trip to Raja Ampat, for me, it stopped being abstract. In one of the few remaining mega biodiverse marine regions on Earth, I witnessed beauty and breakdown sitting side-by-side.

I saw reefs bleached under heat stress, plastic particles bobbing alongside tropical fish in the current, and fishing boats pushing into protected waters already struggling for survival.

What I saw was not an isolated local tragedy. It was a glimpse of our broken covenant with the ocean; total dependence on one side, relentless demands on the other.

Since the industrial revolution, the ocean has both enabled our economic success and shielded us from the worst consequences of our broken economic model.

90% of global trade travels across the ocean. Coastal ecosystems protect trillions in infrastructure and livelihoods. 3.5 billion people rely on the ocean as a primary source of food. Over 80% of global rainfall is driven by oceanic evaporation. And the ocean produces half of all the oxygen we breathe, more than all the world’s forests combined.

It is the connective tissue across the planetary boundaries. Climate stability, biodiversity, freshwater cycles, food security, pollution, land use: all are shaped by ocean health, as is almost everything else from inflation and migration to geopolitical stability and insurance risk.

And since the early 1800s the ocean has quietly absorbed over 90% of the excess heat generated by our factories, our engines, and our relentless appetite for consumption.

Through it all, the ocean has asked for nothing in return.

But now it is breaking its silence.

In just a few decades, we have pushed nearly half the world’s fisheries into overexploitation or collapse. Monitored marine wildlife populations have declined by nearly 50% since 1970.

For all the ocean does to protect life on Earth, it remains astonishingly unprotected by us.

We still govern it as an afterthought, protect it in fragments, and worst of all, actively subsidise its destruction.

Global fisheries subsidies total roughly US$35 billion annually. More than US$22 billion directly fuels industrial overcapacity and overfishing.

And while high seas theoretically belong to everyone, they are not being emptied by everyone.

Just five fishing powers, China, Spain, Taiwan, Japan and South Korea account for more than 85% of observed industrial fishing activity on the high seas.

What looks on a map like open blue space is in reality a giant grey zone where activity is easily hidden and responsibility obscured.

And where environmental accountability fades, human exploitation often follows. The ILO estimates that 128,000 people are trapped in forced labour aboard fishing vessels worldwide, often in truly miserable conditions.

It is hard to imagine a cleaner example of economic irrationality: taxpayers underwrite the destruction of a critical shared asset, while the profits are privatised and the direct costs are carried by coastal communities, small-scale fishers and workers hidden inside supply chains often marked by illegality, coercion, and abuse.

The recent entry into force of a global High Seas Treaty is an important breakthrough. For the first time, the world has a legal framework to protect biodiversity beyond national borders. But treaties without courage, enforcement, and finance will not save the ocean. We cannot negotiate protection during the day while subsidising destruction at night.

So what now?

The first task is simple: stop paying for the damage.

Governments must end the harmful fisheries subsidies that keep destructive fleets at sea, complete a meaningful World Trade Organisation Fish 2 agreement, and turn the High Seas Treaty from legal architecture into living protection. That means finance, enforcement, monitoring, science-based marine protected areas, support for regenerative coastal economies and stronger regional fisheries bodies with the power to act.

But the immediate leverage point is clear. If much of high seas fishing survives only because governments subsidise it, then governments must begin by removing the public money that makes destruction profitable.

If governments fail to act at that scale, more radical options will inevitably move from the margins to the mainstream, including a precautionary pause on all industrial high seas fishing to give ecosystems time to recover.

Food retailers, hospitality groups and seafood companies should buy only seafood that can be fully traced to well-managed fisheries and fish farms. They should eliminate sourcing linked to illegal fishing and forced labour and invest in genuinely regenerative aquaculture. If a company cannot say where its seafood came from, how it was caught, and under what conditions, it should not be selling or serving it.

For too long, we have treated ocean protection as costly and ocean destruction as profitable. This is a dangerous fiction in the way that we measure prosperity.

A depleted ocean means weaker food security, greater climate instability, collapsing livelihoods, more migration pressure, and a poorer, more dangerous world.

The ocean has protected us from the worst consequences of our destructive impulses, but it has now reached its limit.

The question we must now ask ourselves is whether we are finally prepared to protect it in return.

Every subscription to this newsletter helps put a digital Braille learning device into a child’s hands for a day through B1G1 - opening access to education and opportunity. Encourage others to follow this newsletter and be part of becoming Net Positive.
item.dc:creator: Paul Polman

item.title: We Have Confused Liquidation with Wealth Creation
item.description:
This month I attended Venice Climate Week. Sitting by the lagoon, watching the water move against the edge of the city, I was reminded that Venice is not simply vulnerable to the climate crisis, it is a place where the deeper relationship between nature, wealth, and risk is built into every stone, canal, and tide

This city was built in partnership with nature. The lagoon was not a backdrop to the city’s prosperity, it was the condition that made it possible.

It protected the city, it connected it to the world, it empowered this small place to become one of the great centres of commerce, art, finance, and governance in human history.

The city’s success rested on a constant bargain with the lagoon: understand its rhythms, respect its limits, invest in its maintenance, or risk losing the conditions that made the city possible. It became prosperous because it understood that its prosperity rested on these foundations, and that foundations must be maintained.

Today, the waters that carried Venice to wealth and influence are rising against the city itself. Flooding that was once exceptional is becoming more frequent and more dangerous.

Venice shows in miniature what the global economy is now confronting at scale.

For over a century we have built extraordinary prosperity. Life expectancy has doubled, extreme poverty has fallen dramatically, and technology has transformed almost every aspect of human life.

But we have also made a profound accounting error. We have been very good at measuring transactions, and very bad at valuing foundations.

Our economic system counts the timber, but not the forest’s role in holding soil, storing carbon, and regulating water cycles. It counts the hospital bill, but not the value of disease prevention. It counts the cost of rebuilding after a flood, but not the value of stability that would have prevented the flood in the first place. It counts economic activity, but it fails to value the assets that make that activity possible.

And every system optimises for what it measures. If we measure extraction, we get extraction. If we measure regeneration, we get regeneration.

That understanding is beginning to reshape economic thinking around the world.

Today, more than two-thirds of OECD countries have formally incorporated wellbeing indicators into government decision-making, budgeting frameworks, or national performance assessments.

New Zealand has a wellbeing budget. Scotland is accountable to a National Performance Framework that makes the wellbeing of future generations a legal obligation. Bhutan, a country I am excited to visit later in the year, has a GNH index which measures Gross National Happiness.

These are not soft ideas, they are the early architecture of a different accounting system, one that can distinguish between generating value and simply consuming it.

And this matters because we are entering a fundamentally different era. The industrial age was built around scale, the information age was built around connectivity, the next era will be built around resilience.

Stable climates, healthy soils, reliable freshwater, thriving oceans, social trust, shared truth, human health. These are not externalities; they are productive assets. They are forms of capital. And for decades we have been drawing them down while recording the proceeds as growth.

Recomendados pelo LinkedIn

Africa–Gulf: A Practical Alliance for Climate-Ready…
Nasra Nanda Há 8 meses
Vision-Led Development: Still Waiting for the Vision?
Colin Black Há 1 ano
Plus ça change, plus c’est la meme!
Lucy Wood Há 3 meses

Imagine a company selling off its factories, liquidating its intellectual property, firing its workforce, and calling the resulting cash flow “performance.”

No board would tolerate it, no investor would applaud it, yet collectively we have done something remarkably similar on a global scale. We have confused liquidation with wealth creation.

On the surface the model appeared successful. It’s like paying off a credit card with a loan. For a while the household still feels wealthy, the spending continues, the lifestyle is maintained. But the liability has not disappeared. Eventually the bills will arrive.

Today they arrive as climate shocks, as food insecurity, as biodiversity loss, as water stress, as rising health costs, as geopolitical instability, as supply chains that fracture under pressure, as insurance markets retreating from entire regions.

The task now is to build an economy that can tell the difference between growth and liquidation.

That requires a different kind of accounting: one that can tell whether a company is creating real value or simply converting natural, human, and social capital into short-term financial returns; one that understands whether costs are being reduced through innovation or shifted onto workers, suppliers, communities, ecosystems and future generations; one that recognises whether an economy is strengthening the foundations of prosperity, or quietly consuming them.

That is the heart of Net Positive: value creation measured across the full system, not only inside the boundaries of the firm. It is a test of whether business can create value in a world of hard limits.

A Net Positive company gives more than it takes because it understands that long-term performance depends on the health of the systems around it. It succeeds by leaving its suppliers stronger, its customers better served, its communities more resilient, and the natural world more capable of renewal.

That is not idealism, it’s disciplined capitalism for an age of disruption.

Venice became powerful because it understood its dependence on the lagoon. It maintained the channels, adapted to the tides, and respected the natural system that made commerce possible.

The global economy now needs the same wisdom at a planetary scale. We have spent too long counting the proceeds of depletion as wealth. The future belongs to those who can build prosperity without destroying its foundations.

Every subscription to this newsletter helps put a digital Braille learning device into a child’s hands for a day through B1G1 - opening access to education and opportunity. Encourage others to follow this newsletter and be part of becoming Net Positive.
item.dc:creator: Paul Polman

item.title: Democracy Does Not Defend Itself
item.description:
In a recent reflection on America’s 250th anniversary, I wrote about the country’s historic capacity for renewal.

The United States has never been perfect. It's power never lay in perfection, but in reform. Again and again, America found ways to argue with itself, confront its own hypocrisies, widen the circle of dignity, and keep moving - often slowly and incompletely - towards its own highest ideals. That capacity for self-repair became one of the country’s greatest sources of strength.

A newly released book from the Common Sense Coalition, Betrayed: America Didn’t Vote for This, exposes the fragility behind that strength. What happens when the machinery of renewal and repair is itself being systematically dismantled?

The book is not simply another volume in the already crowded literature on Donald Trump. It is a study in national self-sabotage. In how a country can weaken itself under the banner of strength and turn its back on the principles, institutions, and norms that underpin the American experiment itself.

America did not become the world’s most powerful economy simply because it possessed capital, land, technology, or a powerful military. Other nations have controlled vast territory, abundant resources, powerful armies, and formidable technical skills.

America’s distinctive advantage came from the systems and ideologies that allowed human potential to compound over time: the rule of law, independent courts, world-class universities, scientific ambition, deep capital markets, immigration, open inquiry, institutional restraint, and the ability to attract talent from around the world.

It remained powerful because it held open the possibility of progress. It gave people inside and outside the country reason to believe that democracy could evolve, that diversity could strengthen a nation, that debate could lead to reform, and that freedom under law would unleash human potential at extraordinary scale.

But that possibility is being corroded.

The book documents attacks on judges, universities, scientific institutions, civil servants, alliances, climate policy, and democratic guardrails. But the deeper pattern is more disturbing than any single abuse. It is the normalisation of institutional degradation itself, and the replacement of serious governance with cultural warfare, political retaliation, and performative grievance.

This matters morally, but it also matters economically. Independent courts protect contracts. Leading universities attract talent. Well-funded research institutions push the frontier of knowledge. Sustained immigration draws ambition, invention, and risk-taking from across the world. And checks and balances restrain arbitrary power and give confidence that no leader stands above the law.

Recomendados pelo LinkedIn

Presenting the 2025 Policy Lab Reports
Max Bell School Policy Lab Há 12 meses
The Superpower Cleanse: How to Shrink a Nation in 90…
Todd Copilevitz Há 1 ano
An Architect Reads the Articles of Confederation and…
William Schmalz, FAIA, CSI, Há 5 anos

One of the strongest sections of Betrayed focuses on the destruction of American soft power and global alliances. It repeatedly warns that by undermining science, diplomacy, and institutional competence, the United States is actively weakening itself in the defining economic and technological competition of the century.

The attack on climate policy is particularly shortsighted. The transition to a low-carbon economy represents one of the largest economic transformations in modern history. Clean energy, industrial decarbonisation and climate resilience will shape global competitiveness for decades to come. Yet the book describes a government retreating from scientific and industrial leadership precisely when innovation and investment are needed most.

The costs will not be measured only in emissions. They will show up in weaker industries, slower innovation, higher insurance costs, lost export opportunities, and the suffering of millions of individual Americans.

The silence of many U.S. CEOs on these issues only deepens the risk. As my Net Positive co-author Andrew Winston has argued, what may look prudent for one company becomes corrosive at scale, narrowing the space for others to defend democracy, markets, and the rule of law.

In staying quiet, business leaders also misunderstand their own interests. Markets do not thrive on deregulation and tax cuts alone. They depend on confidence, legitimacy, clear rules, talent, open inquiry, and a shared respect for facts. Strip those foundations away and prosperity becomes more brittle, however strong the stock market may appear in the short term.

The deeper lesson of Betrayed is that democracy does not defend itself. Progress does not protect itself. Institutions do not endure on the memory of past success alone. They survive because leaders choose courage over convenience, citizens choose engagement over despair, and businesses understand that freedom, law, truth and trust are the foundations on which prosperity is built.

The world does not need a perfect America; it never had one. But it urgently needs an America that remembers the ideals that make it great, and one that finds again the courage to live up to them again.

Every subscription to this newsletter helps put a digital Braille learning device into a child’s hands for a day through B1G1 - opening access to education and opportunity. Encourage others to follow this newsletter and be part of becoming Net Positive.
item.dc:creator: Paul Polman

item.title: America at 250: The Courage to Reinvent Again
item.description:
Two hundred and fifty years is a long time.

Most political systems do not survive that long.

Most companies certainly do not.

Most empires disappear.

And history has a habit of humbling nations that confuse power with permanence.

Yet the United States remains one of history’s great unfinished experiments.

That is both America’s strength and its challenge.

As the country reflects on its 250th anniversary, the most important question is not whether America has been successful. The evidence of its impact on the modern world is overwhelming.

The real question is whether America can reinvent itself once again for the next 250 years.

Because perhaps the genius of America was never perfection.

Perhaps it was always renewal.

At its best, America also understood something essential: leadership was not domination. It was stewardship.

The country moved forward when it expanded dignity, widened opportunity, and strengthened the belief that more people belonged in the future it was creating.

It struggled when systems became too extractive - economically, socially, politically, or environmentally.

The United States was founded around a radical idea: that human beings possess inherent dignity, that power should be accountable, and that liberty belongs not to kings, but to citizens.

The Declaration of Independence was not merely a political document. It was a moral statement about human possibility.

Of course, America never fully lived up to those ideals. No country does.

A nation proclaiming liberty tolerated slavery.

A republic speaking of equality excluded women and Indigenous peoples.

And even as progress was made through the civil rights movement and generations of courageous activism, the struggle for equal dignity, representation, and justice remains unfinished today.

Many Americans still feel excluded from the full promise of citizenship, whether through race, poverty, geography, or immigration status.

Democracy is weakened whenever people feel unseen, unheard, or unequal before the system.

But the deeper American story was not perfection. It was the capacity for self-correction. The willingness, however imperfect and incomplete, to expand the definition of who belongs.

That capacity for reinvention became one of the greatest forces for progress in modern history.

Over the last 250 years, the United States helped shape modern democracy, scientific advancement, technological innovation, entrepreneurship, philanthropy, higher education, and the post-war international order.

The world’s leading universities. Breakthrough medicines. The internet. Space exploration. Venture capital. Large-scale philanthropy.

Many of these ecosystems flourished because America combined freedom, talent, capital, and openness to new ideas in ways few societies ever have.

At its best, America helped create conditions in which humanity itself could progress.

That is when American leadership became most powerful.

And it is also why so many people around the world, myself included, still care deeply about America.

Part of my family is American. I studied and worked there. Some of the most formative experiences of my life happened there.

What always struck me was not only the scale or ambition of the country. It was the optimism.

The belief that tomorrow could be better than today.

Societies rarely decline first economically. They decline when they lose belief in the future.

And today, many Americans are asking difficult questions about that future.

Questions about inequality, polarization, institutional trust, loneliness, disinformation, and the growing gap between wealth creation and human dignity.

America became exceptionally good at generating wealth, but less successful at distributing dignity.

Over time, parts of the system became increasingly extractive, economically, socially, environmentally, even psychologically.

Perhaps the deepest concern today is not political division itself. Democracies have survived division before.

The deeper danger is fragmentation of shared reality.

Recomendados pelo LinkedIn

Transfer of Power: The Strength of America
Bill Palifka Há 1 ano
Five Undeniable Reasons Why America Remains the Best…
Brendan P. Keegan Há 11 meses
The Next Era of U.S. Influence Won’t Be Loud. It Will…
Josiah U. Há 5 meses

No democracy can function if citizens no longer agree on what is true.

Nor can it thrive when large groups of people feel that the system no longer sees them, protects them, or allows them to fully participate in shaping the future.

Yet strangely, this moment also creates opportunity.

Because America still possesses extraordinary assets: world-class universities, scientific leadership, entrepreneurial dynamism, deep capital markets, philanthropy, creativity, and a remarkable diversity of talent.

The issue is no longer capability.

It is leadership.

It is whether America chooses fear or courage. Extraction or stewardship. Division or shared humanity.

And here, history still offers important lessons.

As Walter Isaacson explained in his recent book “The Greatest Sentence Ever Written” ; George Washington, despite all his flaws, understood something many modern leaders forget. He surrounded himself with talented people without fearing they might outshine him. He helped unite states with profoundly different interests into one country. And most importantly, he gave up power voluntarily - twice. At a moment in history when leaders often clung to power indefinitely, Washington understood that the highest office in a democracy was not president, but citizen.

That idea matters enormously today.

Because the next 250 years of America will not be secured by technology alone. Or military strength. Or GDP growth.

They will depend on whether the country can renew the social contract itself.

Whether Americans can once again celebrate what unites them rather than what divides them.

Whether the circle of belonging continues to widen rather than contract.

Whether people feel they matter.

This may sound soft in an age dominated by AI, geopolitics, and financial markets. It is not.

One of the most important ideas emerging in America today is the concept of “mattering.” People need to feel seen, valued, connected, and capable of contributing to something larger than themselves.

Research from economist Raj Chetty and others shows that upward mobility is shaped not only by economics, but by relationships, aspiration, community, and cross-class human connection.

Agency grows when people believe they matter.

Democracy depends on citizens believing they matter.

Martin Luther King Jr. often said that “the arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice.”

But history also teaches that progress does not bend automatically.

Every generation must choose whether to widen the circle of human dignity or narrow it.

And perhaps that is the deeper challenge of our time: restoring dignity alongside opportunity.

The next American century should not be measured only by economic growth or geopolitical power.

It should be measured by whether America helps humanity navigate the great transitions ahead:

The climate transition, the AI transition, the energy transition, the transition from extraction to stewardship.

Because humanity has entered a new phase of history.

One where interdependence is unavoidable.

Where stewardship becomes survival.

Where leadership requires moral courage as much as economic power.

The world does not need a perfect America.

But it does need an America capable of renewal.

An America confident enough not merely to compete, but to convene.

Not merely to dominate, but to inspire.

Not merely to consume, but to steward.

Perhaps that has always been the real American story.

Not perfection.

Reinvention.

Again and again.
item.dc:creator: Paul Polman

item.title: The Corruption Trap: The Choice Facing US CEOs
item.description:
The lights are flashing red. That’s how Senator Elizabeth Warren described the current state of the U.S. financial system in a recent address to the Council of Institutional Investors, a group who together represent trillions in patient capital that underpins the U.S. economy.

It was an indictment of what she called a “tsunami of corruption” sweeping through American business and government, and a warning to the CEOs lining up to participate in it.

“Because there has been such trust in our markets,” Warren told the New York Times in a preview of the speech, “we can survive some corruption for some period of time, but not this much corruption for very much longer.”

The new normal

On the second day of last year's government shutdown, then Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem rode on horseback at Mount Rushmore to film a 60-second anti-immigration ad. The production was part of a $220 million campaign that cost taxpayers over twice as much as the budget for Christopher Nolan’s Oppenheimer. The majority, $143 million, went to a company called Safe America Media, incorporated less than two weeks before the contract was awarded. It had no website, no prior federal contracts, and no discernible staff. Its registered address was the home of a Republican political operative. The subcontractor that received the work was run by the husband of Noem's own press secretary.

Where lesser scandals once drove weeks of sustained front-page coverage and investigative follow-up, this story moved from headline to background noise in a matter of days.

After Trump said Switzerland's president "just rubbed me the wrong way", tariffs on the country rose to 39%, the highest rate imposed on any developed economy. After a delegation of Swiss billionaires arrived at the White House bearing a gold Rolex desk clock and a one-kilogram personalised gold bar, they fell to 15% shortly afterwards. The sequence was not subtle.

Three days before his inauguration, the Trump family launched a meme coin that generated hundreds of millions of dollars in fees and token sales in its first weeks alone, while creating a mechanism through which special interests and foreign governments could channel money directly to the president of the United States with no oversight and no questions asked.

A listed company, Freight Technologies, filed paperwork with the US securities regulator explaining it was buying $20 million of the coin because it was "an effective way to advocate for fair, balanced, and free trade." This was not a backroom deal or a dark money transfer. It was a public filing.

In February 2025, the administration paused enforcement of the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, the global backstop against corruption, and the SEC has now ceased pursuing cases in this space altogether. Companies previously exposed to overseas corruption investigations gained roughly $39 billion in market capitalisation in a single day on the news.

What the market is actually telling us

Trump's offer to business is simple: play along and the returns will follow. Stand on principle and pay the price.

U.S. CEOs cannot stop what this administration is doing. But they can control how they respond to the invitation. And the evidence suggests the choice they face is not as clear cut as Trump would have them believe.

When Anthropic refused to grant the Pentagon use of its AI for fully autonomous weapons and mass surveillance of US citizens, it did not pay the price Trump's logic predicts. Downloads surged 220%. Daily active users rose 183%. Paid subscribers doubled. Microsoft, one of the largest US government contractors, filed in court alongside them. The market delivered a clear verdict: a values-based stand, far from being penalised, generated reputational returns that translated immediately into commercial ones.

Recomendados pelo LinkedIn

The Silent Tax: The Cost of Corruption - Part IV
Marco Rola Há 8 meses
On Power, Conscience, and the Architecture of Hope:…
Katlego Majola CRM Prof (SA) Há 7 meses
Nurturing Integrity: Instilling Anti-Corruption Values…
Toronata Tambun Há 2 anos

A guide for CEOs

The most powerful thing corporate CEOs can do in this environment is not step back from influence. It is use that influence differently. End self-serving lobbying. Replace it with net positive advocacy. Advocacy that protects the rules, the standards, and the level playing field that allow markets to function, society to thrive, and businesses to compete.

Recognise that before you can change the system, you must have your own house in order. Real and lasting change moves from the inside out, from who you are as a leader, to how your organisation operates, to the coalitions you build, to the systems you help reshape. You cannot credibly advocate for a better world while running a business that acts against that vision.

That means leadership must be unmistakable. Anti-corruption policies must be clear, enforced, and visibly backed from the top. Transparency must be the default: political contributions and lobbying spend disclosed openly, without waiting to be asked. Sunlight remains one of the most effective antidotes to corruption. What cannot withstand disclosure probably should not be happening in the first place.

Stand visibly behind your people. Compliance officers are being asked to hold the line while governments signal that the rules are negotiable. Leaders must make clear that their company's code of conduct does not move with political cycles, and that those who raise concerns will be protected.

Then widen the circle. No single firm can resist systemic pressure alone. Industry coalitions that establish shared standards are the best bet to prevent a race to the bottom. When companies act collectively to defend the enforcement frameworks that make honest competition possible, they are not being idealistic, they are protecting the conditions on which their own survival depends.

And, perhaps most importantly, leaders must speak out publicly. Silence from responsible companies cedes the definition of normal to those with the least interest in maintaining it.

For leaders who remain unpersuaded, just remember, the FCPA pause is not a green light. It is an enforcement holiday, not a repeal. A five-year statute of limitations means conduct today is prosecutable well into the next administration. The UK, Switzerland, and France have already established a joint prosecutorial taskforce to pursue cases the United States declines to bring. The audit trail does not disappear because the current DOJ has temporarily looked away.

The choice

The power of business in politics is real. We have seen what that power deployed for self-interest produces: gold bar diplomacy, meme coins, regulatory exemptions that last a few quarters, if that. The returns are visible, but they are fleeting.

Anthropic used their corporate power differently. The returns were equally real, and they have the potential to compound with time rather than corrode.

Political cycles come and go. The systems that govern markets last far longer. The true test of leadership in this moment comes back to two questions that have always sat at the heart of responsible business. Are you profiting from solving problems, or from creating them? Is the world a better place because your company is in it?

No administration will be in office forever. The executives who have mortgaged their reputations for proximity to this moment will eventually be left holding an empty hand.

Build a company around proximity to power, and it will last exactly as long as that power does. Build it around trust and service to something greater than shareholder value, and you can create a legacy that outlasts any administration.

This is the choice available to leaders right now, but it will not be available forever.

Every subscription to this newsletter helps put a digital Braille learning device into a child’s hands for a day through B1G1 - opening access to education and opportunity. Encourage others to follow this newsletter and be part of becoming Net Positive.
item.dc:creator: Paul Polman
